title: Azzattiwadas
draftNote: Pilot draft — the first 21 clauses. Sign identifications and readings are provisional and still being checked against the corpus and the stones; expect changes.
about: Around 700 BCE, on a hilltop above the Ceyhan river in Cilicia, a local ruler named Azatiwada built a fortress-city and wrote his boast on its gates — twice in Hieroglyphic Luwian and once in Phoenician, the longest text in the hieroglyphs of ancient Anatolia and the key that confirmed their decipherment. He tells how he made his land flourish, filled its granaries, crushed its enemies, and set his king's heir on the throne. A word of honesty: this script was deciphered only in the 20th century, and its sign readings were substantially revised as recently as 1973. This text's readings are the most secure in the whole corpus, because the Phoenician parallel anchors them — but individual words remain scholarly guesses, and the notes say so plainly.
source: KARATEPE 1 (the Azatiwada inscription), Hieroglyphic Luwian version, copy Hu., clauses §1–§21 of ~75. Text (transcription and sign-by-sign transliteration) follows the Annotated Corpus of Luwian Texts, ACLT 2 (I. Yakubovich et al., luwian.web-corpora.net), with sign identifications per the Unicode Anatolian Hieroglyphs repertoire (Laroche numbering); lemma meanings checked against open scholarly literature. The inscription survives in duplicate copies at the citadel's two gates (Hu. and Ho.); §17 is preserved only in Ho. Transliteration conventions: Hawkins-style values as adopted by ACLT (the city name of clause 2 read Hiyawa; others read Adanawa). The stone's word-divider strokes and sign reversals are not reproduced. The original runs boustrophedon (alternating direction line by line) in registers of vertically stacked signs; it is presented here left-to-right and linear, with the corpus's recorded line divisions shown as ‖ marks. Translations are made fresh from the Luwian for this edition. Pronunciation convention: conventional scholarly Iron Age Luwian values (z = ts, h = a rasped kh).

sentences.t: 𔐀𔖻 𔖲𔐓𔖪𔑣𔓯𔗬𔐫𔗦 𔖖𔓚𔖻𔗦 𔐉𔑣𔓯𔗦 𔖖𔓢𔕙𔑯𔗔 𔖷𔗲𔗦
sentences.l: I (am) Azatiwada, the Sun-blessed one, the man, the servant of Tarhunzas,

morph.gloss: I (written with the pointing-figure sign EGO)
sentence particle — function debated; left untranslated
Azatiwada — the ruler who speaks this inscription (his name may mean 'beloved of the Sun')
nominative singular ending — marks the subject
man, person (written with the head sign CAPUT)
Tarhunzas, the Storm-god — head of the Luwian pantheon (written with the thunder sign)

sentences.i: I am Azatiwada — blessed of the Sun, servant of Tarhunzas —
sentences.n: The royal self-presentation that opens the monument. Watch the spelling system work: a logogram carries the word (EGO 'I', SOL 'Sun', CAPUT 'man'), small syllable-signs spell its ending, and unpronounced classifiers mark the categories — DEUS before both divine names, the personal-name marker before Azatiwada. You'll see this pattern in every clause.

sentences.l: whom Awariku made great, the Hiyawan king,

morph.gloss: Awariku — king of Hiyawa (the Urikki of Assyrian records)
quotative particle — hangs on the first word of nearly every clause, marking the running narration; you'll see it constantly
who, which (the relative pronoun, written with the REL sign)
Hiyawan, of Hiyawa — the kingdom in the Cilician plain

sentences.i: whom Awariku, king of Hiyawa, made great.
sentences.n: The clause hangs off §1's 'I': 'whom Awariku made great.' The country's name-sign (*429) is genuinely disputed: the corpus followed here reads Hiyawa (connected by some to 'Ahhiyawa', the Hittite name tied to the Greeks); the standard edition read Adanawa (Adana). The land is the same either way — the Cilician plain.

sentences.t: 𔗬𔑾𔑺 𔖖𔓢𔕙𔖪𔗔 𔐓𔗢𔗬𔓱𔔂 𔑘𔐤𔘟𔐤 𔐞𔑣𔓶 𔓯𔖩𔓯𔐫
sentences.l: And me Tarhunzas made mother and father to Hiyawa,

morph.gloss: me (clitic pronoun, leaning on the first word)
motherhood, a mother (from anna- 'mother', written with the woman sign)
father (accusative; the final -n is left unwritten, as often)
and (clitic — glued to the end of its word)

sentences.i: And Tarhunzas made me a mother and a father to Hiyawa,
sentences.n: The monument's most famous self-description: the ruler as both parents of his land. Note the little words that can't stand alone (=wa, =mu, =ha) leaning on their neighbors — Luwian clauses open with these clitic chains constantly.

sentences.l: And forth I made Hiyawa flourish,

morph.gloss: away, forth, utterly (the preverb ARHA — colors the verb like English 'out' in 'spread out')

sentences.i: And I made Hiyawa prosper,

sentences.t: 𔑁𔓊𔖹𔓶𔓶𔓁 𔐓𔗢𔓁𔖪𔔂 𔓤𔓁𔖱𔖪 𔖩𔐤 𔖬𔓯𔕸𔖻 𔐛𔓱𔐤 𔖩𔕸𔓁 𔓛𔗳𔑶𔑯𔖻𔓯 𔐛𔐤
sentences.l: And I extended the Hiyawan plain, on this side toward the setting (of the sun), and on that side toward the rising,

morph.gloss: extend, widen (classified by the hand sign)
plain, open land (meaning approximate; classified by the earth sign, TERRA+X)
on this side (paired: zin … zin=pa 'on the one side … on the other')
the sunset, the west (classified by the setting-sun sign OCCIDENS)
but, and on the other hand (contrast clitic)
the sunrise, the east (classified by the rising-sun sign ORIENS)

sentences.i: And I enlarged the plain of Hiyawa, westward and eastward alike,
sentences.n: The geography is drawn in pictures: west is the sunset sign (OCCIDENS), east the sunrise sign (ORIENS). The scribe carved the hand classifier on 'extend' reversed on the stone — reversals like this are common and carry no meaning.

sentences.t: 𔐓𔖻𔓱𔖪𔓟𔗬 𔖓𔓶𔒖𔖪 𔐓𔗢𔓁𔓱𔔂 𔖝𔖻𔒄 𔕧𔗔𔐤𔗬𔓱 𔒂𔖢𔖱𔗔 𔒈𔓶𔗔𔗔𔓶 𔐓𔗦𔑯
sentences.l: And in my days there were for Hiyawa all good things, abundance and satiety,

morph.gloss: all, every (OMNIS with attached mi; the ma re-sounds its ending)
abundance, fullness — written under the horn sign, the overflowing horn (meaning approximate)
satiety(?) — classified by the licking-mouth sign; the nuance is a scholarly guess

sentences.i: And in my days Hiyawa had every good thing — plenty and fullness,
sentences.n: 'Abundance' sits under the horn sign and 'satiety' under a licking mouth — the prosperity boast made visible. Both nuances are approximate: the concepts are secured by context, the exact words debated.

sentences.l: And I filled the Paharean granaries,

morph.gloss: fill (classified by the hand sign, carved reversed)
of Pahar — the city Pahri on the Pyramos river
granary (classified by the storehouse sign HORREUM)

sentences.i: And I filled the granaries of Pahar,

sentences.l: And horse upon horse I made,

morph.gloss: horse (accusative; written with the horse's-head sign — the corpus reads the carved form as a compound, EQUUS.ANIMA)
particle of place/motion — untranslatable filler in English

sentences.i: And I amassed horse upon horse,
sentences.n: First of three drumbeat clauses (§8–§10), each with the same figure: 'X upon X I made.' The first 'horse' is spelled logogram-first (horse's head + sound signs), the second purely in sound signs with the horse head demoted to classifier — the same word, two spellings, side by side.

sentences.l: and army upon army I made,
sentences.i: and army upon army,

sentences.l: and shield upon shield I made.

morph.gloss: shield(?) — a word known almost only from this text; the meaning is inferred

sentences.i: and shield upon shield.
sentences.n: 'Shield' (harrali-) is one of this text's near-unique words — the rendering is a scholarly inference from its sign and from the parallel Phoenician version, not a certainty.

sentences.l: But the defiant(?) I utterly crushed(?),

morph.gloss: rebels(?), marauders(?) — the word's meaning is genuinely uncertain
crush(?), destroy(?) — the verb is uncertain; the capital letter in scholars' transcription (makkissaHa) marks a sound the spelling leaves open

sentences.i: But the rebels I crushed utterly,
sentences.n: An honestly murky clause: both the noun and the verb are best guesses. The drift — enemies destroyed — is anchored by the surrounding text; the exact words are not. Reading an inscription deciphered within living memory means sitting with gaps like this.

sentences.l: But the evils which were in the land,

morph.gloss: evils, bad things (classified by the MALUS sign)
land, earth (classified by the TERRA sign)

sentences.i: And as for the evils that were in the land —

sentences.l: them I drove forth out of the land.

morph.gloss: them + the place particle — a whole clitic chain hiding in one modest spelling
ablative ending — 'out of' (the ending is a modern restoration; the stone is worn)
chase, drive — the verb's sign is damaged on the stone; FUGARE 'chase' is the accepted restoration

sentences.i: I drove them out of the land.
sentences.n: The verb's logogram is broken away on the stone — the blank box marks the damage. 'Chase' (FUGARE) is the standard restoration, helped by the parallel copy and the Phoenician text. Damage like this is why every reading here is honestly provisional.

sentences.l: And my lord's house I exalted in goodness,

morph.gloss: lord (DOMINUS) — here 'my lord's', a possessive form
house (written with the house-plan sign DOMUS)

sentences.i: And I made my lord's house flourish,
sentences.n: 'My lord's house' is the royal line of Awariku — Azatiwada presents himself throughout not as usurper but as the dynasty's guardian.

sentences.t: 𔐓𔖻𔓟𔗬 𔖺𔓵𔓯 𔕒𔓶𔖢𔗷 𔖝𔖻𔒄 𔕧𔗔𔐤𔗬𔓱 𔑀𔐤 𔓯𔖩𔓯𔓶
sentences.l: and for my lord's offspring I did all good things with (them).

morph.gloss: offspring, family line (classified by the NEPOS 'descendant' sign; the final small stroke is a scribal mark whose function scholars still debate)

sentences.i: and I did every kindness for my lord's family.

sentences.l: And him I seated upon his father's throne.

morph.gloss: him + the place particle (fused in the spelling)
throne (classified by the THRONUS seat sign)
seat, cause to sit (the SOLIUM 'seat' sign; most of this word is restored — the stone is broken here)

sentences.i: And I set him on his father's throne.
sentences.n: 'Him' is Awariku's heir. The final verb is mostly a restoration (the brackets in scholarly editions); what survives fits 'I seated' and the duplicate copy supports it.

sentences.l: [This clause is missing here.]

morph.gloss: clause §17 is not preserved in this copy of the inscription

sentences.n: The monument carries the text twice, at two gates. In this copy (Hu.) the numbering jumps from §16 to §18 — §17 survives only in the other copy, damaged. A reading of it may be added once its text can be verified.

sentences.t: ▢ 𔓯𔖩𔓯𔐫 𔐓𔖻𔓱𔑣 𔖣𔐤𔑣 𔐓𔖻𔓱𔖱𔓶 𔖂𔐓𔑯𔐤𔗔𔒄𔑣 𔐓𔖻𔓱𔖱𔓟 𔕧𔗔𔐤𔗬𔗔𔖹𔑣
sentences.l: […every king] made [me a father(?)] because of my justice and because of my wisdom and because of my goodness.

morph.gloss: the opening of this clause is broken away
ablative ending (a second form of 'because of')
wisdom — written under the heart sign: wisdom lives in the heart

sentences.i: [Every king] treated me [as a father], for my justice, my wisdom, and my kindness.
sentences.n: The clause opening is broken off in this copy; the sense in brackets is supplied from the duplicate at the other gate ('every king made me a father to himself…'). Note the triple ablative drumming through: because-of-my-justice, because-of-my-wisdom, because-of-my-goodness.

sentences.l: And fortresses I built at the strong places(?) […

morph.gloss: fortress (classified by the fortress-wall sign CASTRUM)
meaning unknown — perhaps 'strongholds' or 'strong places' (one of this text's unique words, under the fist sign)

sentences.i: And I built fortresses in the strong places(?) …
sentences.n: The second word is a true unknown — a hapax, a word found nowhere else — and the clause breaks off at the edge of the damage. Everything after 'I built fortresses' is a guess flagged as a guess.

sentences.t: 𔖟𔐓𔑡𔗬𔖱𔓯𔖩𔗬𔑯 𔐉𔘟𔖩 𔕰𔑯𔐤 𔗷𔑯 𔐓𔗔𔑯 𔓺𔑺𔗔𔒖𔖩
sentences.l: where evil men were, brigands,

morph.gloss: evil, bad (classified by the MALUS sign)
3rd person plural past — '(they) were' (the n goes unwritten)
thieves, brigands (the classifying sign is damaged; the word itself is read securely)

sentences.i: where there had been evil men — brigands —

sentences.t: 𔕵𔓁 𔕰𔖩 𔐾𔐤𔐤 𔐨𔐨𔗲𔑯 𔑾𔗧𔗔𔗔𔐤 𔔙𔓵𔓯
sentences.l: who under the house of Muksas had not served(?),

morph.gloss: served(?), submitted(?) — the double-fist sign's reading is unknown; scholars write the picture's name instead of the word
Muksas — the founding ancestor of the dynasty; the same figure as the Greek seer Mopsos

sentences.i: men who had never submitted to the house of Muksas.
sentences.n: Muksas is one of the monument's marvels: the Phoenician version writes the name MPŠ — the Greek Mopsos, the legendary seer who wandered into Cilicia after the Trojan War. A Greek legend, a Luwian dynasty, a Phoenician spelling, one stone. The verb, written only as two fists, keeps its secret: its pronunciation is unknown.

glossary.ammu: I (written with the pointing-figure sign EGO)
glossary.=mi: sentence particle — function debated; left untranslated
glossary.Azzattiwada-: Azatiwada — the ruler who speaks this inscription (his name may mean 'beloved of the Sun')
glossary.-s: nominative singular ending — marks the subject
glossary.ziti-: man, person (written with the head sign CAPUT)
glossary.Tarhunt-: Tarhunzas, the Storm-god — head of the Luwian pantheon (written with the thunder sign)
glossary.Awarku-: Awariku — king of Hiyawa (the Urikki of Assyrian records)
glossary.=wa: quotative particle — hangs on the first word of nearly every clause, marking the running narration; you'll see it constantly
glossary.kwi-: who, which (the relative pronoun, written with the REL sign)
glossary.Ahhiyawanni-: Hiyawan, of Hiyawa — the kingdom in the Cilician plain
glossary.=mu: me (clitic pronoun, leaning on the first word)
glossary.annatti-: motherhood, a mother (from anna- 'mother', written with the woman sign)
glossary.tadi-: father (accusative; the final -n is left unwritten, as often)
glossary.=ha: and (clitic — glued to the end of its word)
glossary.ahha: away, forth, utterly (the preverb ARHA — colors the verb like English 'out' in 'spread out')
glossary.latra-: extend, widen (classified by the hand sign)
glossary.warra-: plain, open land (meaning approximate; classified by the earth sign, TERRA+X)
glossary.zin: on this side (paired: zin … zin=pa 'on the one side … on the other')
glossary.ibami-: the sunset, the west (classified by the setting-sun sign OCCIDENS)
glossary.=pa: but, and on the other hand (contrast clitic)
glossary.kistami-: the sunrise, the east (classified by the rising-sun sign ORIENS)
glossary.tanim(a)-: all, every (OMNIS with attached mi; the ma re-sounds its ending)
glossary.suri-: abundance, fullness — written under the horn sign, the overflowing horn (meaning approximate)
glossary.hassa-: satiety(?) — classified by the licking-mouth sign; the nuance is a scholarly guess
glossary.suwa-: fill (classified by the hand sign, carved reversed)
glossary.Paharawanni-: of Pahar — the city Pahri on the Pyramos river
glossary.karuna-: granary (classified by the storehouse sign HORREUM)
glossary.azzu-: horse (accusative; written with the horse's-head sign — the corpus reads the carved form as a compound, EQUUS.ANIMA)
glossary.=tta: particle of place/motion — untranslatable filler in English
glossary.harrali-: shield(?) — a word known almost only from this text; the meaning is inferred
glossary.mariyanni-: rebels(?), marauders(?) — the word's meaning is genuinely uncertain
glossary.makkissa-: crush(?), destroy(?) — the verb is uncertain; the capital letter in scholars' transcription (makkissaHa) marks a sound the spelling leaves open
glossary.haniyatta-: evils, bad things (classified by the MALUS sign)
glossary.taskwar-: land, earth (classified by the TERRA sign)
glossary.=ada=tta: them + the place particle — a whole clitic chain hiding in one modest spelling
glossary.-ari: ablative ending — 'out of' (the ending is a modern restoration; the stone is worn)
glossary.parha-: chase, drive — the verb's sign is damaged on the stone; FUGARE 'chase' is the accepted restoration
glossary.nanni-: lord (DOMINUS) — here 'my lord's', a possessive form
glossary.parna-: house (written with the house-plan sign DOMUS)
glossary.hassu-: offspring, family line (classified by the NEPOS 'descendant' sign; the final small stroke is a scribal mark whose function scholars still debate)
glossary.=an=tta: him + the place particle (fused in the spelling)
glossary.isatra-: throne (classified by the THRONUS seat sign)
glossary.isnuwa-: seat, cause to sit (the SOLIUM 'seat' sign; most of this word is restored — the stone is broken here)
glossary.[missing clause]: clause §17 is not preserved in this copy of the inscription
glossary.attanassamma-: wisdom — written under the heart sign: wisdom lives in the heart
glossary.harnissa-: fortress (classified by the fortress-wall sign CASTRUM)
glossary.allummida-: meaning unknown — perhaps 'strongholds' or 'strong places' (one of this text's unique words, under the fist sign)
glossary.attuwari-: evil, bad (classified by the MALUS sign)
glossary.-nta: 3rd person plural past — '(they) were' (the n goes unwritten)
glossary.usalli-: thieves, brigands (the classifying sign is damaged; the word itself is read securely)
glossary.(PUGNUS.PUGNUS)li-: served(?), submitted(?) — the double-fist sign's reading is unknown; scholars write the picture's name instead of the word
glossary.Muksa-: Muksas — the founding ancestor of the dynasty; the same figure as the Greek seer Mopsos
